title: Ecstasy (drug)

infobox.value: 3,4-MDMA; Ecstasy (E, X, XTC); Midomafetamine; Molly; Mandy;[[2]](#cite_note-Luciano_2014-2)[[3]](#cite_note-DrugFacts-3) Pingers/Pingas[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[Entactogen](/wiki/Entactogen "Entactogen"); [Stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant"); [Psychedelic](/wiki/Serotonergic_psychedelic "Serotonergic psychedelic"); [Serotonin–norepinephrine–dopamine releasing agent](/wiki/Serotonin%E2%80%93norepinephrine%E2%80%93dopamine_releasing_agent "Serotonin–norepinephrine–dopamine releasing agent"); [5-HT2 receptor](/wiki/5-HT2_receptor "5-HT2 receptor") [agonist](/wiki/Agonist "Agonist")

* AU: + S8 (PTSD)   + S9 (all other uses) * BR: [Class F2](/wiki/Brazilian_Controlled_Drugs_and_Substances_Act#Class_F2 "Brazilian Controlled Drugs and Substances Act") (Prohibited psychotropics)[[12]](#cite_note-12) * CA: [Schedule I](/wiki/Controlled_Drugs_and_Substances_Act#Schedule_I "Controlled Drugs and Substances Act") * DE: [Anlage I](/wiki/Drugs_controlled_by_the_German_Narcotic_Drugs_Act#Anlage_I "Drugs controlled by the German Narcotic Drugs Act") (Authorized scientific use only) * NZ: [Class B](/wiki/Misuse_of_Drugs_Act_1975#Class_B "Misuse of Drugs Act 1975") * UK: [Class A](/wiki/Drugs_controlled_by_the_UK_Misuse_of_Drugs_Act#Class_A_drugs "Drugs controlled by the UK Misuse of Drugs Act") * US: [Schedule I](/wiki/Controlled_Substances_Act#Schedule_I_drugs "Controlled Substances Act") * UN: [Psychotropic Schedule I](/wiki/Convention_on_Psychotropic_Substances#Schedule_I "Convention on Psychotropic Substances") * EU: Fully prohibited * SE: Förteckning I

(**MDMA**), commonly known as **ecstasy** (tablet form), and **molly** (crystal form),[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) is an [entactogen](/wiki/Entactogen "Entactogen") with [stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant") and minor [psychedelic](/wiki/Psychedelic_drug "Psychedelic drug") properties.[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[21]](#cite_note-Green_2003-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Ecstasy (drug)](/w/index.php?title=Ecstasy_(drug)&redirect=no "Ecstasy (drug)"))

Psychoactive drug, often called ecstasy

For other uses, see [MDMA (disambiguation)](/wiki/MDMA_(disambiguation) "MDMA (disambiguation)"). Not to be confused with [MDA (drug)](/wiki/MDA_(drug) "MDA (drug)"); [EDMA](/wiki/EDMA "EDMA"); [NMDA](/wiki/NMDA "NMDA"); [2,3-MDMA](/wiki/2,3-MDMA "2,3-MDMA"); or [MDNA](/wiki/MDNA "MDNA").

Pharmaceutical compound

MDMA  
INN: Midomafetamine[[1]](#cite_note-INN-1)

**3,4-Methylenedioxymethamphetamine** (**MDMA**), commonly known as **ecstasy** (tablet form), and **molly** (crystal form),[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) is an [entactogen](/wiki/Entactogen "Entactogen") with [stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant") and minor [psychedelic](/wiki/Psychedelic_drug "Psychedelic drug") properties.[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[21]](#cite_note-Green_2003-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)

MDMA was first synthesized in 1912 by [Merck](/wiki/Merck_Group "Merck Group") chemist [Anton Köllisch](/wiki/Anton_K%C3%B6llisch "Anton Köllisch").[[23]](#cite_note-FreudenmannÖxlerBernschneider-Reif2006-23) It was used to enhance psychotherapy beginning in the 1970s and became popular as a street drug in the 1980s.[[24]](#cite_note-Drugs2014-24)[[25]](#cite_note-NIH2016-25) MDMA is commonly associated with [dance parties](/wiki/Dance_party "Dance party"), [raves](/wiki/Rave "Rave"), and [electronic dance music](/wiki/Electronic_dance_music "Electronic dance music").[[26]](#cite_note-WHO2004-26) Tablets sold as ecstasy may be [mixed](/wiki/Cutting_agent "Cutting agent") with other substances such as [ephedrine](/wiki/Ephedrine "Ephedrine"), [amphetamine](/wiki/Amphetamine "Amphetamine"), and [methamphetamine](/wiki/Methamphetamine "Methamphetamine").[[24]](#cite_note-Drugs2014-24) In 2016, about 21 million people between the ages of 15 and 64 used ecstasy (0.3% of the world population).[[27]](#cite_note-UN2018-27) In the United States, as of 2017, about 7% of people have used MDMA at some point in their lives and 0.9% have used it in the last year.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The lethal risk from one dose of MDMA is estimated to be from 1 death in 20,000 instances to 1 death in 50,000 instances.[[29]](#cite_note-White_2014-29)

The purported pharmacological effects that may be [prosocial](/wiki/Prosocial_behavior "Prosocial behavior") include altered sensations, increased energy, empathy, and pleasure.[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)[[24]](#cite_note-Drugs2014-24) When taken by mouth, effects begin in 30 to 45 minutes and last three to six hours.[[13]](#cite_note-Freye_2009-13)[[25]](#cite_note-NIH2016-25) Short-term adverse effects include [grinding of the teeth](/wiki/Bruxism "Bruxism"), [blurred vision](/wiki/Blurred_vision "Blurred vision"), [sweating](/wiki/Sweating "Sweating"), and a [rapid heartbeat](/wiki/Tachycardia "Tachycardia"),[[24]](#cite_note-Drugs2014-24) and extended use can also lead to addiction, [memory problems](/wiki/Amnesia "Amnesia"), [paranoia](/wiki/Paranoia "Paranoia"), and [difficulty sleeping](/wiki/Insomnia "Insomnia"). Deaths have been reported due to increased body temperature and dehydration. MDMA acts primarily by increasing the release of the [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitters "Neurotransmitters") [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"), [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"), and [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine") in parts of the brain.[[24]](#cite_note-Drugs2014-24)[[25]](#cite_note-NIH2016-25) It belongs to the [substituted amphetamine](/wiki/Substituted_amphetamine "Substituted amphetamine") [classes of drugs](/wiki/Chemical_classification "Chemical classification").[[10]](#cite_note-EU2015-10)[[30]](#cite_note-30) MDMA is structurally similar to [mescaline](/wiki/Mescaline "Mescaline") (a psychedelic), methamphetamine (a stimulant), as well as [endogenous](/wiki/Endogeny_(biology) "Endogeny (biology)") [monoamine neurotransmitters](/wiki/Monoamine_neurotransmitter "Monoamine neurotransmitter") such as serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

MDMA has limited approved medical uses in a small number of countries,[[32]](#cite_note-32) but is illegal in most jurisdictions.[[33]](#cite_note-33) [MDMA-assisted psychotherapy](/wiki/MDMA-assisted_psychotherapy "MDMA-assisted psychotherapy") is a promising and generally safe treatment for [post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder") when administered in controlled therapeutic settings.[[34]](#cite_note-:1-34)[[35]](#cite_note-:2-35) In the United States, the [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration") (FDA) has given MDMA [breakthrough therapy](/wiki/Breakthrough_therapy "Breakthrough therapy") status (though there no current clinical indications in the US).[[36]](#cite_note-36) Canada has allowed limited distribution of MDMA upon application to and approval by [Health Canada](/wiki/Health_Canada "Health Canada").[[37]](#cite_note-37) In Australia, it may be prescribed in the treatment of PTSD by specifically authorised psychiatrists.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Uses

### Recreational

MDMA is often considered the drug of choice within the [rave](/wiki/Rave "Rave") culture and is also used at clubs, festivals, and [house parties](/wiki/House_party "House party").[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15) In the rave environment, the sensory effects of music and lighting are often highly [synergistic](/wiki/Synergy "Synergy") with the drug. The psychedelic [amphetamine](/wiki/Amphetamine "Amphetamine") quality of MDMA offers multiple appealing aspects to users in the rave setting. Some users enjoy the feeling of mass communion from the inhibition-reducing effects of the drug, while others use it as party fuel because of the drug's stimulatory effects.[[39]](#cite_note-39) MDMA is used less often than other stimulants, typically less than once per week.[[40]](#cite_note-Epstein2013-40)

MDMA is sometimes taken in conjunction with other psychoactive drugs such as [LSD](/wiki/LSD "LSD"),[[41]](#cite_note-:0-41) [psilocybin mushrooms](/wiki/Psilocybin_mushroom "Psilocybin mushroom"), [2C-B](/wiki/2C-B "2C-B"), and [ketamine](/wiki/Ketamine "Ketamine"). The combination with LSD is called "candy-flipping".[[41]](#cite_note-:0-41) The combination with 2C-B is called "nexus flipping". For this combination, most people take the MDMA first, wait until the peak is over, and then take the 2C-B.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

MDMA is often co-administered with [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)"), [methamphetamine](/wiki/Methamphetamine "Methamphetamine"), and prescription drugs such as [SSRIs](/wiki/Selective_serotonin_reuptake_inhibitor "Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor") with which MDMA has several drug-drug interactions.[[43]](#cite_note-Hysek_2012-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Three life-threatening reports of MDMA co-administration with [ritonavir](/wiki/Ritonavir "Ritonavir") have been reported;[[46]](#cite_note-46) with ritonavir having severe and dangerous drug-drug interactions with a wide range of both psychoactive, anti-psychotic, and non-psychoactive drugs.[[47]](#cite_note-Carpenter_2019-47)

### Medical

See also: [MDMA § Research](#Research), and [MDMA-assisted psychotherapy](/wiki/MDMA-assisted_psychotherapy "MDMA-assisted psychotherapy")

Before it was widely banned, it saw limited use in psychotherapy.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[10]](#cite_note-EU2015-10)[[48]](#cite_note-48) In 2017 the [United States Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/United_States_Food_and_Drug_Administration "United States Food and Drug Administration") (FDA) granted breakthrough therapy designation for MDMA-assisted psychotherapy for [post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder") (PTSD).[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)

MDMA has limited approved medical uses in a small number of countries,[[51]](#cite_note-51) but is illegal in most jurisdictions.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Canada has allowed limited distribution of MDMA upon application to and approval by [Health Canada](/wiki/Health_Canada "Health Canada").[[53]](#cite_note-53) In Australia, it may be prescribed in the treatment of PTSD by specifically authorised psychiatrists.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Some researchers have proposed that psychedelics in general may act as [active](/wiki/Active_placebo "Active placebo") "super [placebos](/wiki/Placebo "Placebo")" used for therapeutic purposes.[[55]](#cite_note-Dupuis_2022-55)[[56]](#cite_note-van_Elk_2022-56)

### Others

Small doses of MDMA are used by some religious practitioners as an [entheogen](/wiki/Entheogen "Entheogen") to enhance prayer or meditation.[[57]](#cite_note-MDMA_and_Religion-57) MDMA has been used as an adjunct to [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age") spiritual practices.[[58]](#cite_note-Watson_&_Beck-58)

### Forms

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/1g_MDMA-HCl.jpg/250px-1g_MDMA-HCl.jpg)](/wiki/File:1g_MDMA-HCl.jpg)

1 gram of pure MDMA hydrochloride crystals

MDMA has become widely known as ecstasy (shortened "E", "X", or "XTC"), usually referring to its tablet form, although this term may also include the presence of possible [adulterants](/wiki/Adulterant "Adulterant") or diluents. The UK term "mandy" and the US term "molly" colloquially refer to MDMA in a crystalline powder form that is thought to be free of adulterants.[[2]](#cite_note-Luciano_2014-2)[[3]](#cite_note-DrugFacts-3)[[59]](#cite_note-DEA_2015_assessment-59) MDMA is also sold in the form of the hydrochloride salt, either as loose crystals or in [gelcaps](/wiki/Capsule_(pharmacy) "Capsule (pharmacy)").[[60]](#cite_note-Molly_S05E07-60)[[61]](#cite_note-Molly_S06E05-61) MDMA tablets can sometimes be found in a shaped form that may depict characters from [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture"). These are sometimes collectively referred to as "fun tablets".[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Partly due to the global supply shortage of [sassafras oil](/wiki/Sassafras_oil "Sassafras oil")—a problem largely assuaged by use of improved or alternative modern methods of synthesis—the purity of substances sold as molly have been found to vary widely. Some of these substances contain [methylone](/wiki/Methylone "Methylone"), [ethylone](/wiki/Ethylone "Ethylone"), [MDPV](/wiki/MDPV "MDPV"), [mephedrone](/wiki/Mephedrone "Mephedrone"), or any other of the group of compounds commonly known as [bath salts](/wiki/Bath_salts_(drug) "Bath salts (drug)"), in addition to, or in place of, MDMA.[[3]](#cite_note-DrugFacts-3)[[59]](#cite_note-DEA_2015_assessment-59)[[60]](#cite_note-Molly_S05E07-60)[[61]](#cite_note-Molly_S06E05-61) Powdered MDMA ranges from pure MDMA to crushed tablets with 30–40% purity.[[10]](#cite_note-EU2015-10) MDMA tablets typically have low purity due to bulking agents that are added to dilute the drug and increase profits (notably lactose) and binding agents.[[10]](#cite_note-EU2015-10) Tablets sold as ecstasy sometimes contain [3,4-methylenedioxyamphetamine](/wiki/3,4-methylenedioxyamphetamine "3,4-methylenedioxyamphetamine") (MDA), [3,4-methylenedioxyethylamphetamine](/wiki/3,4-methylenedioxyethylamphetamine "3,4-methylenedioxyethylamphetamine") (MDEA), other amphetamine derivatives, caffeine, opiates, or painkillers.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8) Some tablets contain little or no MDMA.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[10]](#cite_note-EU2015-10)[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64) The proportion of seized ecstasy tablets with MDMA-like impurities has varied annually and by country.[[10]](#cite_note-EU2015-10) The average content of MDMA in a preparation is 70 to 120 mg with the purity having increased since the 1990s.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)

MDMA is usually consumed by mouth. It is also sometimes [snorted](/wiki/Insufflation_(medicine) "Insufflation (medicine)").[[24]](#cite_note-Drugs2014-24)

## Effects

In general, MDMA users report feeling the onset of subjective effects within 30 to 60 minutes of oral consumption and reaching peak effect at 75 to 120 minutes, which then plateaus for about 3.5 hours.[[65]](#cite_note-mcn-65) The desired short-term psychoactive effects of MDMA have been reported to include:

* [Euphoria](/wiki/Euphoria "Euphoria") – a sense of general [well-being](/wiki/Well-being "Well-being") and happiness[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)
* Increased self-confidence, sociability, and perception of facilitated communication[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)
* [Entactogenic](/wiki/Entactogen "Entactogen") effects—increased [empathy](/wiki/Empathy "Empathy") or feelings of closeness with others[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66) and oneself[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)
* [Dilated pupils](/wiki/Mydriasis "Mydriasis")[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)
* Relaxation and reduced anxiety[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)
* Increased emotionality[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)
* A sense of inner peace[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)
* Mild hallucination[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)
* Enhanced sensation, perception, or sexuality[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)
* Altered sense of time[[25]](#cite_note-NIH2016-25)

The experience elicited by MDMA depends on the dose, setting, and user.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8) The variability of the induced altered state is lower compared to other psychedelics. For example, MDMA used at parties is associated with high motor activity, reduced sense of identity, and poor awareness of surroundings. Use of MDMA individually or in small groups in a quiet environment and when concentrating, is associated with increased lucidity, concentration, sensitivity to aesthetic aspects of the environment, enhanced awareness of emotions, and improved capability of communication.[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15)[[67]](#cite_note-Landriscina-67) In psychotherapeutic settings, MDMA effects have been characterized by infantile ideas, mood lability, and memories and moods connected with childhood experiences.[[67]](#cite_note-Landriscina-67)[[68]](#cite_note-Baggott_2015-68)

MDMA has been described as an "[empathogenic](/wiki/Empathogen "Empathogen")" drug because of its empathy-producing effects.[[69]](#cite_note-Schmid_2014-69)[[70]](#cite_note-Wardle_2014-70) Results of several studies show the effects of increased empathy with others.[[69]](#cite_note-Schmid_2014-69) When testing MDMA for medium and high doses, it showed increased hedonic and arousal continuum.[[71]](#cite_note-Bravo_2001-71)[[72]](#cite_note-Metzner_2005-72) The effect of MDMA increasing sociability is consistent, while its effects on empathy have been more mixed.[[73]](#cite_note-KamilarBritt_2015-73)

## Side effects

### Short-term

Acute adverse effects are usually the result of high or multiple doses, although single dose toxicity can occur in susceptible individuals.[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22) The most serious short-term physical health risks of MDMA are [hyperthermia](/wiki/Hyperthermia "Hyperthermia") and [dehydration](/wiki/Dehydration "Dehydration").[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[74]](#cite_note-Keane_2014-74) Cases of life-threatening or fatal [hyponatremia](/wiki/Hyponatremia "Hyponatremia") (excessively low sodium concentration in the blood) have developed in MDMA users attempting to prevent dehydration by [consuming excessive amounts of water](/wiki/Water_intoxication "Water intoxication") without replenishing [electrolytes](/wiki/Electrolytes "Electrolytes").[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[74]](#cite_note-Keane_2014-74)[[29]](#cite_note-White_2014-29)

The immediate adverse effects of MDMA use can include:

* [Bruxism](/wiki/Bruxism "Bruxism") (grinding and clenching of the teeth)[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15)[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)
* [Dehydration](/wiki/Dehydration "Dehydration")[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15)[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[74]](#cite_note-Keane_2014-74)
* [Diarrhea](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)
* [Erectile dysfunction](/wiki/Erectile_dysfunction "Erectile dysfunction")[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[75]](#cite_note-75)
* [Hyperthermia](/wiki/Hyperthermia "Hyperthermia")[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15)[[74]](#cite_note-Keane_2014-74)
* Increased wakefulness or [insomnia](/wiki/Insomnia "Insomnia")[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)
* Increased perspiration and sweating[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[74]](#cite_note-Keane_2014-74)
* Increased [heart rate](/wiki/Heart_rate "Heart rate") and [blood pressure](/wiki/Blood_pressure "Blood pressure")[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15)[[74]](#cite_note-Keane_2014-74)
* Increased [psychomotor](/wiki/Psychomotor_agitation "Psychomotor agitation") activity[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)
* Loss of [appetite](/wiki/Appetite "Appetite")[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)
* Nausea and vomiting[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)
* Visual and auditory hallucinations (rarely)[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)

Other adverse effects that may occur or persist for up to a week following cessation of moderate MDMA use include:[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)

Physiological

* Insomnia[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)
* Loss of appetite[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)
* Tiredness or lethargy[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)
* [Trismus](/wiki/Trismus "Trismus") (lockjaw)[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)

Psychological

* [Anhedonia](/wiki/Anhedonia "Anhedonia")[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)
* Anxiety or paranoia[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)
* Depression[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)
* Impulsiveness[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)
* Irritability[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)
* Memory impairment[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)
* Restlessness[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)

### Long-term

As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=MDMA&action=edit), the long-term effects of MDMA on human brain structure and function have not been fully determined.[[78]](#cite_note-Abstinent_MDMA_fMRI_review-78) However, there is consistent evidence of structural and functional deficits in MDMA users with high lifetime exposure.[[78]](#cite_note-Abstinent_MDMA_fMRI_review-78) These structural or functional changes appear to be dose dependent and may be less prominent in MDMA users with only a moderate (typically <50 doses used and <100 tablets consumed) lifetime exposure. Nonetheless, moderate MDMA use may still be [neurotoxic](/wiki/Neurotoxicity "Neurotoxicity") and what constitutes moderate use is not clearly established.[[79]](#cite_note-Mueller_2016-79)

Furthermore, it is not clear yet whether "typical" recreational users of MDMA (1 to 2 pills of 75 to 125 mg MDMA or analogue every 1 to 4 weeks) will develop neurotoxic brain lesions.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Long-term exposure to MDMA in humans has been shown to produce marked [neurodegeneration](/wiki/Neurodegeneration "Neurodegeneration") in [striatal](/wiki/Striatal "Striatal"), [hippocampal](/wiki/Hippocampal "Hippocampal"), [prefrontal](/wiki/Prefrontal_cortex "Prefrontal cortex"), and [occipital](/wiki/Occipital_cortex "Occipital cortex") serotonergic [axon terminals](/wiki/Axon_terminal "Axon terminal").[[78]](#cite_note-Abstinent_MDMA_fMRI_review-78)[[81]](#cite_note-Meth_MDMA_NTox-81) Neurotoxic damage to serotonergic axon terminals has been shown to persist for more than two years.[[81]](#cite_note-Meth_MDMA_NTox-81) Elevations in brain temperature from MDMA use are positively correlated with MDMA-induced neurotoxicity.[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15)[[78]](#cite_note-Abstinent_MDMA_fMRI_review-78)[[79]](#cite_note-Mueller_2016-79) However, most studies on MDMA and serotonergic neurotoxicity in humans focus more on heavy users who consume as much as seven times or more the amount that most users report taking. The evidence for the presence of serotonergic neurotoxicity in casual users who take lower doses less frequently is not conclusive.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

However, adverse [neuroplastic](/wiki/Neuroplastic "Neuroplastic") changes to brain [microvasculature](/wiki/Microvasculature "Microvasculature") and [white matter](/wiki/White_matter "White matter") have been observed to occur in humans using low doses of MDMA.[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15)[[78]](#cite_note-Abstinent_MDMA_fMRI_review-78) Reduced [gray matter](/wiki/Gray_matter "Gray matter") density in certain brain structures has also been noted in human MDMA users.[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15)[[78]](#cite_note-Abstinent_MDMA_fMRI_review-78) Global reductions in gray matter volume, thinning of the parietal and orbitofrontal cortices, and decreased hippocampal activity have been observed in long term users.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8) The effects established so far for recreational use of ecstasy lie in the range of moderate to severe effects for [serotonin transporter](/wiki/Serotonin_transporter "Serotonin transporter") reduction.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

Impairments in multiple aspects of cognition, including attention, learning, memory, [visual processing](/wiki/Role_of_serotonin_in_visual_orientation_processing#MDMA_and_Visual_Orientation_Processing "Role of serotonin in visual orientation processing"), and sleep, have been found in regular MDMA users.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)[[84]](#cite_note-Pharm2014-84)[[78]](#cite_note-Abstinent_MDMA_fMRI_review-78) The magnitude of these impairments is correlated with lifetime MDMA usage[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)[[84]](#cite_note-Pharm2014-84)[[78]](#cite_note-Abstinent_MDMA_fMRI_review-78) and are partially reversible with abstinence.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8) Several forms of memory are impaired by chronic ecstasy use;[[22]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013-22)[[84]](#cite_note-Pharm2014-84) however, the effects for memory impairments in ecstasy users are generally small overall.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-Meta_analysis_-_MDMA_memory_impairment-86) MDMA use is also associated with increased impulsivity and depression.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)

Serotonin depletion following MDMA use can cause depression in subsequent days. In some cases, depressive symptoms persist for longer periods.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8) Some studies indicate repeated recreational use of ecstasy is associated with depression and anxiety, even after quitting the drug.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Depression is one of the main reasons for cessation of use.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)

At high doses, MDMA induces a [neuroimmune response](/wiki/Neuroimmune_system "Neuroimmune system") that, through several mechanisms, increases the permeability of the [blood–brain barrier](/wiki/Blood%E2%80%93brain_barrier "Blood–brain barrier"), thereby making the brain more susceptible to environmental toxins and [pathogens](/wiki/Pathogen "Pathogen").[[88]](#cite_note-MDMA_BBB-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] In addition, MDMA has [immunosuppressive](/wiki/Immunosuppressive "Immunosuppressive") effects in the [peripheral nervous system](/wiki/Peripheral_nervous_system "Peripheral nervous system") and pro-inflammatory effects in the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system").[[90]](#cite_note-90)

MDMA may increase the risk of [cardiac valvulopathy](/wiki/Cardiac_valvulopathy "Cardiac valvulopathy") in heavy or long-term users due to activation of serotonin [5-HT2B receptors](/wiki/5-HT2B_receptor "5-HT2B receptor").[[91]](#cite_note-Cavero_2014-91)[[92]](#cite_note-Padhariya_2017-92) MDMA induces cardiac [epigenetic changes](/wiki/Epigenetics "Epigenetics") in [DNA methylation](/wiki/DNA_methylation "DNA methylation"), particularly hypermethylation changes.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

### Reinforcement disorders

Approximately 60% of MDMA users experience [withdrawal](/wiki/Drug_withdrawal "Drug withdrawal") symptoms when they stop taking MDMA.[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64) Some of these symptoms include fatigue, loss of appetite, depression, and trouble concentrating.[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64) [Tolerance](/wiki/Drug_tolerance "Drug tolerance") to some of the desired and adverse effects of MDMA is expected to occur with consistent MDMA use.[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64) A 2007 [delphic analysis](/wiki/Delphi_method "Delphi method") of a panel of experts in pharmacology, psychiatry, law, policing and others estimated MDMA to have a psychological dependence and physical dependence potential roughly three-fourths to four-fifths that of cannabis.[[94]](#cite_note-Nutt_2007-94)

MDMA has been shown to induce [ΔFosB](/wiki/%CE%94FosB "ΔFosB") in the [nucleus accumbens](/wiki/Nucleus_accumbens "Nucleus accumbens").[[95]](#cite_note-MDMA_ΔFosB-95) Because MDMA releases dopamine in the [striatum](/wiki/Striatum "Striatum"), the mechanisms by which it induces ΔFosB in the nucleus accumbens are analogous to other dopaminergic psychostimulants.[[95]](#cite_note-MDMA_ΔFosB-95)[[96]](#cite_note-Robison_2011-96) Therefore, chronic use of MDMA at high doses can result in [altered brain structure](/wiki/Neuroplasticity "Neuroplasticity") and [drug addiction](/wiki/Drug_addiction "Drug addiction") that occur as a consequence of ΔFosB overexpression in the nucleus accumbens.[[96]](#cite_note-Robison_2011-96) MDMA is less addictive than other stimulants such as methamphetamine and cocaine.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) Compared with amphetamine, MDMA and its metabolite MDA are less reinforcing.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

One study found approximately 15% of chronic MDMA users met the [DSM-IV](/wiki/DSM-IV "DSM-IV") diagnostic criteria for [substance dependence](/wiki/Substance_dependence "Substance dependence").[[100]](#cite_note-Steinkellner_2011-100) However, there is little evidence for a specific diagnosable MDMA dependence syndrome because MDMA is typically used relatively infrequently.[[40]](#cite_note-Epstein2013-40)

There are currently no medications to treat MDMA addiction.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

### During pregnancy

MDMA is a moderately [teratogenic drug](/wiki/Teratogenic_drug "Teratogenic drug") (i.e., it is toxic to the fetus).[[102]](#cite_note-Vorhees_1997-102)[[103]](#cite_note-Meamar_2010-103) [In utero](/wiki/Uterus "Uterus") exposure to MDMA is associated with a [neuro](/wiki/Neurotoxicity "Neurotoxicity")- and [cardiotoxicity](/wiki/Cardiotoxicity "Cardiotoxicity")[[103]](#cite_note-Meamar_2010-103) and impaired motor functioning. Motor delays may be temporary during infancy or long-term. The severity of these developmental delays increases with heavier MDMA use.[[84]](#cite_note-Pharm2014-84)[[104]](#cite_note-singer-104) MDMA has been shown to promote the survival of fetal dopaminergic neurons in culture.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

## Overdose

MDMA [overdose](/wiki/Overdose "Overdose") symptoms vary widely due to the involvement of multiple [organ systems](/wiki/Organ_system "Organ system"). Some of the more overt overdose symptoms are listed in the table below. The number of instances of fatal MDMA intoxication is low relative to its usage rates. In most fatalities, MDMA was not the only drug involved. Acute toxicity is mainly caused by [serotonin syndrome](/wiki/Serotonin_syndrome "Serotonin syndrome") and [sympathomimetic](/wiki/Sympathomimetic "Sympathomimetic") effects.[[100]](#cite_note-Steinkellner_2011-100) MDMA's toxicity in overdose may be exacerbated by [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine"), with which it is frequently cut in order to increase volume.[[106]](#cite_note-106) The estimated fatal dose of MDMA in humans is around 15 or 16 times a typical recreational dose.[[107]](#cite_note-Henríquez-HernándezRojas-HernándezQuintana-Hernández2023-107) Sympathomimetic side effects can be managed with [carvedilol](/wiki/Carvedilol "Carvedilol").[[108]](#cite_note-Richards_2015-108)[[109]](#cite_note-Hysek_2012b-109) A scheme for management of acute MDMA toxicity has been published focusing on treatment of hyperthermia, hyponatraemia, serotonin syndrome, and multiple organ failure.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

Symptoms of overdose

| System | Minor or moderate overdose[[111]](#cite_note-de_la_Torre_2004-111) | Severe overdose[[111]](#cite_note-de_la_Torre_2004-111) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Cardiovascular](/wiki/Cardiovascular_system "Cardiovascular system") |  | * [Disseminated intravascular coagulation](/wiki/Disseminated_intravascular_coagulation "Disseminated intravascular coagulation")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66) * [Intracranial hemorrhage](/wiki/Intracranial_hemorrhage "Intracranial hemorrhage")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66) * Severe [hypertension](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[112]](#cite_note-Kellum_2008-112) or [hypotension](/wiki/Hypotension "Hypotension")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66) * Hypotensive bleeding[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8) |
| [Central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") | * [Hyperreflexia](/wiki/Hyperreflexia "Hyperreflexia")[[111]](#cite_note-de_la_Torre_2004-111) * [Agitation](/wiki/Psychomotor_agitation "Psychomotor agitation")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[112]](#cite_note-Kellum_2008-112) * [Mental confusion](/wiki/Mental_confusion "Mental confusion")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66) * [Paranoia](/wiki/Paranoia "Paranoia")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[112]](#cite_note-Kellum_2008-112) * [Stimulant psychosis](/wiki/Stimulant_psychosis "Stimulant psychosis")[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15) | * [Cognitive and memory impairment](/wiki/Cognitive_deficit "Cognitive deficit")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66) potentially to the point of [retrograde](/wiki/Retrograde_amnesia "Retrograde amnesia") or [anterograde amnesia](/wiki/Anterograde_amnesia "Anterograde amnesia")[[113]](#cite_note-113) * Coma[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15)[[112]](#cite_note-Kellum_2008-112) * [Convulsions](/wiki/Convulsion "Convulsion")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[112]](#cite_note-Kellum_2008-112) * [Hallucinations](/wiki/Hallucination "Hallucination")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[112]](#cite_note-Kellum_2008-112) * [Loss of consciousness](/wiki/Loss_of_consciousness "Loss of consciousness")[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15) * [Serotonin syndrome](/wiki/Serotonin_syndrome "Serotonin syndrome")[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15)[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[29]](#cite_note-White_2014-29) |
| [Musculoskeletal](/wiki/Musculoskeletal_system "Musculoskeletal system") |  | * [Muscle rigidity](/wiki/Hypertonia "Hypertonia")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66) * [Rhabdomyolysis](/wiki/Rhabdomyolysis "Rhabdomyolysis") (i.e., rapid muscle breakdown)[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[29]](#cite_note-White_2014-29) |
| [Respiratory](/wiki/Respiratory_system "Respiratory system") |  | * [Acute respiratory distress syndrome](/wiki/Acute_respiratory_distress_syndrome "Acute respiratory distress syndrome")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66) |
| [Urinary](/wiki/Urogenital_system "Urogenital system") |  | * [Acute kidney injury](/wiki/Acute_kidney_injury "Acute kidney injury")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[114]](#cite_note-114) |
| Other |  | * [Cerebral edema](/wiki/Cerebral_edema "Cerebral edema")[[15]](#cite_note-pmid22392347-15) * [Hepatitis](/wiki/Hepatitis "Hepatitis")[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[29]](#cite_note-White_2014-29) * [Hyperpyrexia](/wiki/Hyperpyrexia "Hyperpyrexia") (a life-threatening elevation of body temperature greater than or equal to 40.0 or 41.5 °C (104.0 or 106.7 °F))[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[29]](#cite_note-White_2014-29) * [Hyponatremia](/wiki/Hyponatremia "Hyponatremia") ([syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone](/wiki/Syndrome_of_inappropriate_antidiuretic_hormone "Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone"))[[66]](#cite_note-Acute_amph_toxicity-66)[[74]](#cite_note-Keane_2014-74)[[29]](#cite_note-White_2014-29) |

## Interactions

See also: [Trip killer § Antidotes of other hallucinogens](/wiki/Trip_killer#Antidotes_of_other_hallucinogens "Trip killer"), and [MDMA/citalopram](/wiki/MDMA/citalopram "MDMA/citalopram")

A number of [drug interactions](/wiki/Drug_interactions "Drug interactions") can occur between MDMA and other drugs, including [serotonergic](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") drugs.[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64)[[115]](#cite_note-115) MDMA also interacts with drugs which inhibit [CYP450](/wiki/CYP450 "CYP450") enzymes, like [ritonavir](/wiki/Ritonavir "Ritonavir") (Norvir), particularly [CYP2D6](/wiki/CYP2D6 "CYP2D6") inhibitors.[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64) Life-threatening reactions and death have occurred in people who took MDMA while on ritonavir.[[116]](#cite_note-Papaseit_2020-116) [Bupropion](/wiki/Bupropion "Bupropion"), a strong CYP2D6 inhibitor, has been found to increase MDMA exposure with administration of MDMA.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[118]](#cite_note-Schmid_2015-118) Concurrent use of MDMA with certain other serotonergic drugs can result in a life-threatening condition called [serotonin syndrome](/wiki/Serotonin_syndrome "Serotonin syndrome").[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64) Severe overdose resulting in death has also been reported in people who took MDMA in combination with certain [monoamine oxidase inhibitors](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor "Monoamine oxidase inhibitor") (MAOIs),[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)[[64]](#cite_note-Toxnet_MDMA_after-effects-64) such as [phenelzine](/wiki/Phenelzine "Phenelzine") (Nardil), [tranylcypromine](/wiki/Tranylcypromine "Tranylcypromine") (Parnate), or [moclobemide](/wiki/Moclobemide "Moclobemide") (Aurorix, Manerix).[[119]](#cite_note-119) [Serotonin reuptake inhibitors](/wiki/Serotonin_reuptake_inhibitor "Serotonin reuptake inhibitor") (SRIs) such as [citalopram](/wiki/Citalopram "Citalopram") (Celexa), [duloxetine](/wiki/Duloxetine "Duloxetine") (Cymbalta), [fluoxetine](/wiki/Fluoxetine "Fluoxetine") (Prozac), and [paroxetine](/wiki/Paroxetine "Paroxetine") (Paxil) have been shown to block most of the subjective effects of MDMA.[[120]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2020-120) [Norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors](/wiki/Norepinephrine_reuptake_inhibitor "Norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor") (NRIs) such as [reboxetine](/wiki/Reboxetine "Reboxetine") (Edronax) have been found to reduce [emotional excitation](/wiki/Emotional_excitation "Emotional excitation") and feelings of [stimulation](/wiki/Psychostimulant "Psychostimulant") with MDMA but do not appear to influence its [entactogenic](/wiki/Entactogen "Entactogen") or [mood-elevating](/wiki/Euphoriant "Euphoriant") effects.[[120]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2020-120)

MDMA [induces the release of monoamine neurotransmitters](/wiki/Monoamine_releasing_agent "Monoamine releasing agent") and thereby acts as an indirectly acting [sympathomimetic](/wiki/Sympathomimetic "Sympathomimetic") and produces a variety of [cardiostimulant](/wiki/Cardiostimulant "Cardiostimulant") effects.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117) It [dose-dependently](/wiki/Dose_dependence "Dose dependence") increases [heart rate](/wiki/Heart_rate "Heart rate"), [blood pressure](/wiki/Blood_pressure "Blood pressure"), and [cardiac output](/wiki/Cardiac_output "Cardiac output").[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[121]](#cite_note-Mendelson_2012-121) SRIs like citalopram and [paroxetine](/wiki/Paroxetine "Paroxetine"), as well as the [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") [5-HT2A receptor](/wiki/5-HT2A_receptor "5-HT2A receptor") [antagonist](/wiki/Receptor_antagonist "Receptor antagonist") [ketanserin](/wiki/Ketanserin "Ketanserin"), have been found to partially block the increases in heart rate and blood pressure with MDMA.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[122]](#cite_note-Liechti_2000-122) It is notable in this regard that [serotonergic psychedelics](/wiki/Serotonergic_psychedelic "Serotonergic psychedelic") such as [psilocybin](/wiki/Psilocybin "Psilocybin"), which act as serotonin 5-HT2A receptor agonists, likewise have sympathomimetic effects.[[123]](#cite_note-Wso_2023-123)[[124]](#cite_note-Neumann_2024-124)[[125]](#cite_note-Ley_2023-125) The NRI [reboxetine](/wiki/Reboxetine "Reboxetine") and the [serotonin–norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor](/wiki/Serotonin%E2%80%93norepinephrine_reuptake_inhibitor "Serotonin–norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor") (SNRI) duloxetine block MDMA-induced increases in heart rate and blood pressure.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117) Conversely, bupropion, a [norepinephrine–dopamine reuptake inhibitor](/wiki/Norepinephrine%E2%80%93dopamine_reuptake_inhibitor "Norepinephrine–dopamine reuptake inhibitor") (NDRI) with only weak [dopaminergic](/wiki/Dopaminergic "Dopaminergic") activity,[[126]](#cite_note-Hart_2024-126)[[127]](#cite_note-Eap_2021-127) reduced MDMA-induced heart rate and circulating [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine") increases but did not affect MDMA-induced blood pressure increases.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[118]](#cite_note-Schmid_2015-118) On the other hand, the robust NDRI [methylphenidate](/wiki/Methylphenidate "Methylphenidate"), which has sympathomimetic effects of its own, has been found to augment the cardiovascular effects and increases in circulating norepinephrine and [epinephrine](/wiki/Epinephrine "Epinephrine") levels induced by MDMA.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[128]](#cite_note-Hysek_2014-128)

The [non-selective](/wiki/Binding_selectivity "Binding selectivity") [beta blocker](/wiki/Beta_blocker "Beta blocker") [pindolol](/wiki/Pindolol "Pindolol") blocked MDMA-induced increases in heart rate but not blood pressure.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[108]](#cite_note-Richards_2015-108)[[129]](#cite_note-Hysek_2010-129) The [α2-adrenergic receptor](/wiki/%CE%912-adrenergic_receptor "Α2-adrenergic receptor") [agonist](/wiki/Agonist "Agonist") [clonidine](/wiki/Clonidine "Clonidine") did not affect the cardiovascular effects of MDMA, though it reduced blood pressure.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[108]](#cite_note-Richards_2015-108)[[130]](#cite_note-Hysek_2012a-130) The [α1-adrenergic receptor](/wiki/%CE%911-adrenergic_receptor "Α1-adrenergic receptor") antagonists [doxazosin](/wiki/Doxazosin "Doxazosin") and [prazosin](/wiki/Prazosin "Prazosin") blocked or reduced MDMA-induced blood pressure increases but augmented MDMA-induced heart rate and cardiac output increases.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[108]](#cite_note-Richards_2015-108)[[131]](#cite_note-Hysek_2013-131)[[121]](#cite_note-Mendelson_2012-121) The dual α1- and [β-adrenergic receptor](/wiki/%CE%92-adrenergic_receptor "Β-adrenergic receptor") blocker [carvedilol](/wiki/Carvedilol "Carvedilol") reduced MDMA-induced heart rate and blood pressure increases.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[108]](#cite_note-Richards_2015-108)[[109]](#cite_note-Hysek_2012b-109) In contrast to the cases of serotonergic and noradrenergic agents, the [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") [D2 receptor](/wiki/D2_receptor "D2 receptor") antagonist [haloperidol](/wiki/Haloperidol "Haloperidol") did not affect the cardiovascular responses to MDMA.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[132]](#cite_note-Liechti_2000a-132) Due to the theoretical risk of "unopposed α-stimulation" and possible consequences like [coronary vasospasm](/wiki/Coronary_vasospasm "Coronary vasospasm"), it has been suggested that dual α1- and β-adrenergic receptor antagonists like carvedilol and [labetalol](/wiki/Labetalol "Labetalol"), rather than selective beta blockers, should be used in the management of stimulant-induced [sympathomimetic](/wiki/Sympathomimetic "Sympathomimetic") [toxicity](/wiki/Toxicity "Toxicity"), for instance in the context of [overdose](/wiki/Overdose "Overdose").[[108]](#cite_note-Richards_2015-108)[[133]](#cite_note-Richards_2017-133)

## Pharmacology

### Pharmacodynamics

See also: [Entactogen § Mechanism of action](/wiki/Entactogen#Mechanism_of_action "Entactogen"), [Serotonin releasing agent § Effects and comparisons](/wiki/Serotonin_releasing_agent#Effects_and_comparisons "Serotonin releasing agent"), and [Monoamine releasing agent § Mechanism of action](/wiki/Monoamine_releasing_agent#Mechanism_of_action "Monoamine releasing agent")

MDMA is an [entactogen](/wiki/Entactogen "Entactogen") or [empathogen](/wiki/Empathogen "Empathogen"), as well as a [stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant"), [euphoriant](/wiki/Euphoriant "Euphoriant"), and weak [psychedelic](/wiki/Psychedelic_drug "Psychedelic drug").[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[146]](#cite_note-Nichols_2022-146) It is a [substrate](/wiki/Substrate_(biochemistry) "Substrate (biochemistry)") of the [monoamine transporters](/wiki/Monoamine_transporter "Monoamine transporter") (MATs) and acts as a [monoamine releasing agent](/wiki/Monoamine_releasing_agent "Monoamine releasing agent") (MRA).[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[147]](#cite_note-Docherty_2021-147)[[148]](#cite_note-Rothman_2003-148)[[149]](#cite_note-Rothman_2006-149) The drug is specifically a well-balanced [serotonin–norepinephrine–dopamine releasing agent](/wiki/Serotonin%E2%80%93norepinephrine%E2%80%93dopamine_releasing_agent "Serotonin–norepinephrine–dopamine releasing agent") (SNDRA).[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[147]](#cite_note-Docherty_2021-147)[[148]](#cite_note-Rothman_2003-148)[[149]](#cite_note-Rothman_2006-149) To a lesser extent, MDMA also acts as a [serotonin–norepinephrine–dopamine reuptake inhibitor](/wiki/Serotonin%E2%80%93norepinephrine%E2%80%93dopamine_reuptake_inhibitor "Serotonin–norepinephrine–dopamine reuptake inhibitor") (SNDRI).[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[147]](#cite_note-Docherty_2021-147)[[148]](#cite_note-Rothman_2003-148) MDMA enters [monoaminergic](/wiki/Monoaminergic "Monoaminergic") [neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") via the MATs and then, via poorly understood [mechanisms](/wiki/Mechanism_of_action "Mechanism of action"), reverses the direction of these transporters to produce [efflux](/wiki/Efflux_pump "Efflux pump") of the [monoamine neurotransmitters](/wiki/Monoamine_neurotransmitter "Monoamine neurotransmitter") rather than the usual [reuptake](/wiki/Reuptake "Reuptake").[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[150]](#cite_note-Sulzer_2005-150)[[151]](#cite_note-Reith_2020-151)[[152]](#cite_note-Vaughan_2024-152)[[153]](#cite_note-CaseyHeifets2025-153) Induction of monoamine efflux by [amphetamines](/wiki/Amphetamine-type_stimulant "Amphetamine-type stimulant") in general may involve [intracellular](/wiki/Intracellular "Intracellular") [Na+](/wiki/Sodium_ion "Sodium ion") and [Ca2+](/wiki/Calcium_ion "Calcium ion") elevation and [PKC](/wiki/Protein_kinase_C "Protein kinase C") and [CaMKIIα](/wiki/CaMKII%CE%B1 "CaMKIIα") activation.[[150]](#cite_note-Sulzer_2005-150)[[151]](#cite_note-Reith_2020-151)[[152]](#cite_note-Vaughan_2024-152) MDMA also acts on the [vesicular monoamine transporter 2](/wiki/Vesicular_monoamine_transporter_2 "Vesicular monoamine transporter 2") (VMAT2) on [synaptic vesicles](/wiki/Synaptic_vesicle "Synaptic vesicle") to increase the [cytosolic](/wiki/Cytosol "Cytosol") concentrations of the monoamine neurotransmitters available for efflux.[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[147]](#cite_note-Docherty_2021-147) By inducing release and reuptake inhibition of [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"), [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"), and [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"), MDMA increases levels of these neurotransmitters in the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") and [periphery](/wiki/Peripheral_nervous_system "Peripheral nervous system") and thereby indirectly activates the [receptors](/wiki/Receptor_(biochemistry) "Receptor (biochemistry)") of these neurotransmitters.[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[147]](#cite_note-Docherty_2021-147)[[153]](#cite_note-CaseyHeifets2025-153) There are species differences in the balance of MAT activities of MDMA, with it showing greater influence on serotonin in rodents than in humans.[[153]](#cite_note-CaseyHeifets2025-153)

In addition to its actions as an SNDRA, MDMA directly interacts with a number of [monoamine](/wiki/Monoamine_receptor "Monoamine receptor") and other [receptors](/wiki/Receptor_(biochemistry) "Receptor (biochemistry)").[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[134]](#cite_note-PDSPKiDatabase-134)[[135]](#cite_note-BindingDB-135)[[137]](#cite_note-Ray_2010-137)[[136]](#cite_note-Jain_2025-136) With varying [potencies](/wiki/Potency_(pharmacology) "Potency (pharmacology)") ranging from high to low, it is a [partial agonist](/wiki/Partial_agonist "Partial agonist") of several of the serotonin [5-HT1 receptors](/wiki/5-HT1_receptor "5-HT1 receptor"), including the serotonin [5-HT1A](/wiki/5-HT1A_receptor "5-HT1A receptor"), [5-HT1B](/wiki/5-HT1B_receptor "5-HT1B receptor"), and [5-HT1D receptors](/wiki/5-HT1D_receptor "5-HT1D receptor"), and is a partial agonist of the serotonin [5-HT2 receptors](/wiki/5-HT2_receptor "5-HT2 receptor"), including the serotonin [5-HT2A](/wiki/5-HT2A_receptor "5-HT2A receptor"), [5-HT2B](/wiki/5-HT2B_receptor "5-HT2B receptor"), and [5-HT2C receptors](/wiki/5-HT2C_receptor "5-HT2C receptor").[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[136]](#cite_note-Jain_2025-136)[[154]](#cite_note-Pitts_2018-154)[[155]](#cite_note-Setola_2003-155)[[156]](#cite_note-Nash_1994-156) The drug also interacts with [α2-adrenergic receptors](/wiki/Alpha-2_adrenergic_receptor "Alpha-2 adrenergic receptor"), with the [sigma](/wiki/Sigma_receptor "Sigma receptor") [σ1](/wiki/Sigma-1_receptor "Sigma-1 receptor") and [σ2 receptors](/wiki/Sigma-2_receptor "Sigma-2 receptor"), with the [imidazoline](/wiki/Imidazoline_receptor "Imidazoline receptor") [I1 receptor](/wiki/I1_receptor "I1 receptor"), and with other [targets](/wiki/Biological_target "Biological target").[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[134]](#cite_note-PDSPKiDatabase-134)[[135]](#cite_note-BindingDB-135)[[136]](#cite_note-Jain_2025-136)[[137]](#cite_note-Ray_2010-137) Along with the preceding receptor interactions, MDMA is a potent partial agonist of the rodent [trace amine-associated receptor 1](/wiki/Trace_amine-associated_receptor_1 "Trace amine-associated receptor 1") (TAAR1).[[143]](#cite_note-Gainetdinov_2018-143)[[144]](#cite_note-Simmler_2016-144) Conversely however, due to species differences, it is far weaker in terms of potency as an agonist of the human TAAR1.[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[143]](#cite_note-Gainetdinov_2018-143)[[144]](#cite_note-Simmler_2016-144)[[157]](#cite_note-Lewin_2011-157) The drug appears to act as a weak partial agonist of the human TAAR1 rather than as an [efficacious](/wiki/Intrinsic_activity "Intrinsic activity") agonist.[[143]](#cite_note-Gainetdinov_2018-143)[[144]](#cite_note-Simmler_2016-144) In relation to the preceding findings, MDMA has been said to be essentially inactive as a human TAAR1 agonist.[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17) TAAR1 activation is thought to auto-inhibit and constrain the effects of amphetamines that possess TAAR1 agonism, for instance MDMA in rodents.[[147]](#cite_note-Docherty_2021-147)[[158]](#cite_note-Espinoza_2014-158)[[159]](#cite_note-Kuropka_2023-159)[[138]](#cite_note-Simmler_2013-138)[[160]](#cite_note-Di_Cara_2011-160)

Elevation of serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine levels by MDMA is believed to mediate most of the drug's effects, including its entactogenic, stimulant, euphoriant, [hyperthermic](/wiki/Hyperthermia "Hyperthermia"), and [sympathomimetic](/wiki/Sympathomimetic "Sympathomimetic") effects.[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[147]](#cite_note-Docherty_2021-147)[[161]](#cite_note-Rein_2024-161)[[73]](#cite_note-KamilarBritt_2015-73) The entactogenic effects of MDMA, including increased [sociability](/wiki/Sociability "Sociability"), [empathy](/wiki/Empathy "Empathy"), [feelings of closeness](/wiki/Emotional_intimacy "Emotional intimacy"), and [reduced aggression](/wiki/Antiaggressive "Antiaggressive"), are thought to be mainly due to induction of serotonin release.[[73]](#cite_note-KamilarBritt_2015-73)[[120]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2020-120)[[18]](#cite_note-Oeri_2021-18) The exact [serotonin receptors](/wiki/Serotonin_receptor "Serotonin receptor") responsible for MDMA's entactogenic effects are unclear, but may include the serotonin 5-HT1A receptor,[[162]](#cite_note-Esaki_2023-162) 5-HT1B receptor,[[163]](#cite_note-Heifets_2019-163) and 5-HT2A receptor,[[164]](#cite_note-Pitts_2017-164) as well as 5-HT1A receptor-mediated [oxytocin](/wiki/Oxytocin "Oxytocin") release and consequent activation of the [oxytocin receptor](/wiki/Oxytocin_receptor "Oxytocin receptor").[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[73]](#cite_note-KamilarBritt_2015-73)[[165]](#cite_note-BlancoGandia_2015-165)[[166]](#cite_note-Heifets_2024-166)[[146]](#cite_note-Nichols_2022-146)[[153]](#cite_note-CaseyHeifets2025-153) Induction of dopamine release is thought to be importantly involved in the stimulant and euphoriant effects of MDMA,[[153]](#cite_note-CaseyHeifets2025-153)[[17]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2018-17)[[154]](#cite_note-Pitts_2018-154)[[167]](#cite_note-Kaur_2023-167) while induction of norepinephrine release and serotonin 5-HT2A receptor stimulation are believed to mediate its sympathomimetic effects.[[117]](#cite_note-Fonseca_2021-117)[[147]](#cite_note-Docherty_2021-147) Activation of serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT2A receptors is also thought to be involved in the stimulant and euphoriant effects of MDMA, while serotonin 5-HT2C receptor activation is thought to constrain these effects and limit MDMA's [reinforcing potential](/wiki/Drug_addiction "Drug addiction").[[153]](#cite_note-CaseyHeifets2025-153)[[168]](#cite_note-Bankson_2001-168)[[169]](#cite_note-Baumann_2008-169)[[170]](#cite_note-Bankson_2002-170)[[171]](#cite_note-Cole_2003-171)[[172]](#cite_note-Pomrenze_2025-172)[[173]](#cite_note-Canal_2017-173) Serotonin 5-HT2B receptor signaling appears to be required for MDMA-induced serotonin release and effects.[[153]](#cite_note-CaseyHeifets2025-153)[[174]](#cite_note-MartinezPrice_2002-174)[[175]](#cite_note-Stove_2010-175)[[176]](#cite_note-Doly_2008-176)[[177]](#cite_note-Doly_2009-177)[[178]](#cite_note-Belmer_2018-178) MDMA has been associated with a unique subjective "magic" or [euphoria](/wiki/Euphoria "Euphoria") that few or no other known entactogens are said to fully reproduce.[[179]](#cite_note-Baggott_2023-179)[[180]](#cite_note-Baggott2024-180) The mechanisms underlying this property of MDMA are unknown, but it has been theorized to be due to a specific mixture and balance of pharmacological activities, including combined serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine release and direct serotonin receptor agonism.[[181]](#cite_note-Rothman_2002-181)[[179]](#cite_note-Baggott_2023-179)[[180]](#cite_note-Baggott2024-180)[[182]](#cite_note-Luethi_2020-182)

MDMA is often said to have mild or weak [psychedelic](/wiki/Psychedelic_drug "Psychedelic drug") effects.[[183]](#cite_note-Liechti_2001-183)[[120]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2020-120)[[18]](#cite_note-Oeri_2021-18)[[184]](#cite_note-Schenk_2018-184) These effects are said to be [dose-dependent](/wiki/Dose_dependence "Dose dependence"), such that greater [hallucinogenic](/wiki/Hallucinogen "Hallucinogen") effects are produced at higher doses.[[183]](#cite_note-Liechti_2001-183)[[185]](#cite_note-Puxty_2017-185) The mild hallucinogenic effects of MDMA include [perceptual changes](/wiki/Hallucination "Hallucination") like intensification of [visual](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception"), [auditory](/wiki/Auditory_perception "Auditory perception"), and [tactile perception](/wiki/Tactile_perception "Tactile perception") (e.g., brightened colors), a state of [dissociation](/wiki/Dissociation_(psychology) "Dissociation (psychology)") with feelings of [depersonalization](/wiki/Depersonalization "Depersonalization") and [derealization](/wiki/Derealization "Derealization") (e.g., "oceanic boundlessness"), and [thinking disturbances](/wiki/Thought_disorder "Thought disorder").[[183]](#cite_note-Liechti_2001-183)[[186]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013a-186)[[120]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2020-120)[[184]](#cite_note-Schenk_2018-184)[[122]](#cite_note-Liechti_2000-122)[[187]](#cite_note-Liechti_2000b-187)[[185]](#cite_note-Puxty_2017-185) Conversely, overt [hallucinations](/wiki/Hallucination "Hallucination") do not occur, MDMA's hallucinogenic effects are described as "non-problematic" for users, and are said to be much less than those of [3,4-methylenedioxyamphetamine](/wiki/3,4-methylenedioxyamphetamine "3,4-methylenedioxyamphetamine") (MDA) or especially those of fully effective [serotonergic psychedelics](/wiki/Serotonergic_psychedelic "Serotonergic psychedelic") like [psilocybin](/wiki/Psilocybin "Psilocybin").[[186]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013a-186)[[122]](#cite_note-Liechti_2000-122)[[18]](#cite_note-Oeri_2021-18) The hallucinogenic effects of MDMA have been theorized to be mediated by serotonin 5-HT2A receptor activation analogously to the case of classical psychedelics.[[183]](#cite_note-Liechti_2001-183)[[122]](#cite_note-Liechti_2000-122)[[185]](#cite_note-Puxty_2017-185)[[188]](#cite_note-Simmler_2018-188)[[186]](#cite_note-Meyer_2013a-186)[[16]](#cite_note-Straumann_2024-16) Accordingly, the serotonin 5-HT2A receptor [antagonist](/wiki/Antagonist "Antagonist") ketanserin has been reported to reduce MDMA-induced perceptual changes in humans.[[183]](#cite_note-Liechti_2001-183)[[120]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2020-120)[[122]](#cite_note-Liechti_2000-122)[[185]](#cite_note-Puxty_2017-185) Conversely however, it failed to affect MDMA-induced feelings of dissociation and oceanic boundlessness.[[183]](#cite_note-Liechti_2001-183)[[120]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2020-120)[[185]](#cite_note-Puxty_2017-185) In contrast, the serotonin reuptake inhibitor [citalopram](/wiki/Citalopram "Citalopram"), which blocks MDMA-induced serotonin release, diminished all of the psychoactive and hallucinogenic effects of MDMA.[[183]](#cite_note-Liechti_2001-183)[[120]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2020-120)[[187]](#cite_note-Liechti_2000b-187)[[122]](#cite_note-Liechti_2000-122) It has been noted that *N*-[methylation](/wiki/Methylation "Methylation") of psychedelic [phenethylamines](/wiki/Substituted_phenethylamine "Substituted phenethylamine"), as in the [structural](/wiki/Chemical_structure "Chemical structure") difference between MDA and MDMA, has invariably greatly reduced or abolished their psychedelic activity.[[189]](#cite_note-Nichols_2018-189)[[190]](#cite_note-Jacob_1994-190) Whereas MDA and psychedelics like psilocybin induce the [head-twitch response](/wiki/Head-twitch_response "Head-twitch response") in rodents, a behavioral proxy of psychedelic effects, findings on MDMA and the head-twitch response are mixed and conflicting.[[191]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2018-191)[[192]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2022-192)[[120]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2020-120) In addition, whereas MDA fully substitutes for psychedelics like [LSD](/wiki/LSD "LSD") and [DOM](/wiki/DOM_(drug) "DOM (drug)") in rodent [drug discrimination](/wiki/Drug_discrimination "Drug discrimination") tests, MDMA does not do so, nor do psychedelics generally fully substitute for MDMA.[[193]](#cite_note-Nichols_2016-193)[[120]](#cite_note-Halberstadt_2020-120)[[194]](#cite_note-Nichols_1989-194)[[195]](#cite_note-Nichols_1990-195)

Long-term repeated activation of serotonin 5-HT2B receptors by MDMA is thought to result in increased risk of organ complications such as [valvular heart disease](/wiki/Valvular_heart_disease "Valvular heart disease") (VHD) and [primary pulmonary hypertension](/wiki/Primary_pulmonary_hypertension "Primary pulmonary hypertension") (PPH).[[196]](#cite_note-McIntyre_2023-196)[[197]](#cite_note-Tagen_2023-197)[[123]](#cite_note-Wso_2023-123)[[198]](#cite_note-Rothman_2009-198)[[181]](#cite_note-Rothman_2002-181)[[199]](#cite_note-Rothman_2002a-199) MDMA has been associated with [serotonergic neurotoxicity](/wiki/Serotonergic_neurotoxicity "Serotonergic neurotoxicity").[[200]](#cite_note-Costa_2022-200)[[18]](#cite_note-Oeri_2021-18)[[201]](#cite_note-Sprague_1998-201) This may be due to formation of toxic MDMA [metabolites](/wiki/Metabolite "Metabolite") and/or induction of [simultaneous serotonin and dopamine release](/wiki/Serotonin%E2%80%93norepinephrine%E2%80%93dopamine_releasing_agent "Serotonin–norepinephrine–dopamine releasing agent"), with consequent uptake of dopamine into serotonergic neurons and breakdown into [toxic species](/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species "Reactive oxygen species").[[200]](#cite_note-Costa_2022-200)[[18]](#cite_note-Oeri_2021-18)[[201]](#cite_note-Sprague_1998-201) Serotonin 5-HT2 receptor agonists or serotonergic psychedelics may potentiate the neurotoxicity of MDMA.[[202]](#cite_note-GreenMechanElliott2003-202)[[203]](#cite_note-GudelskyYamamotoNash1994-203)[[204]](#cite_note-SchmidtBlackAbbate1990-204)[[205]](#cite_note-Armstrong_2004-205)

MDMA is a [racemic mixture](/wiki/Racemic_mixture "Racemic mixture") of two [enantiomers](/wiki/Enantiomers "Enantiomers"), (*S*)-MDMA and [(*R*)-MDMA](/wiki/(R)-MDMA "(R)-MDMA").[[154]](#cite_note-Pitts_2018-154)[[16]](#cite_note-Straumann_2024-16) (*S*)-MDMA is much more potent as an SNDRA *[in vitro](/wiki/In_vitro "In vitro")* and in producing MDMA-like subjective effects in humans than (*R*)-MDMA.[[154]](#cite_note-Pitts_2018-154)[[149]](#cite_note-Rothman_2006-149)[[16]](#cite_note-Straumann_2024-16)[[206]](#cite_note-Anderson_1978-206) By contrast, (*R*)-MDMA acts as a lower-potency [serotonin–norepinephrine releasing agent](/wiki/Serotonin%E2%80%93norepinephrine_releasing_agent "Serotonin–norepinephrine releasing agent") (SNRA) with weak or negligible effects on dopamine.[[154]](#cite_note-Pitts_2018-154)[[149]](#cite_note-Rothman_2006-149)[[207]](#cite_note-Acquas_2007-207) Relatedly, (*R*)-MDMA shows weak or negligible stimulant-like and [rewarding](/wiki/Reward_system "Reward system") effects in animals.[[154]](#cite_note-Pitts_2018-154)[[208]](#cite_note-Curry_2018-208) Both (*S*)-MDMA and (*R*)-MDMA produce entactogen-type effects in animals and humans.[[154]](#cite_note-Pitts_2018-154)[[16]](#cite_note-Straumann_2024-16) In addition, both (*S*)-MDMA and (*R*)-MDMA are weak agonists of the serotonin 5-HT2 receptors.[[154]](#cite_note-Pitts_2018-154)[[167]](#cite_note-Kaur_2023-167)[[16]](#cite_note-Straumann_2024-16)[[155]](#cite_note-Setola_2003-155)[[156]](#cite_note-Nash_1994-156) (*R*)-MDMA is more potent and efficacious as a serotonin 5-HT2A and 5-HT2B receptor agonist than (*S*)-MDMA, whereas (*S*)-MDMA is somewhat more potent as an agonist of the serotonin 5-HT2C receptor.[[154]](#cite_note-Pitts_2018-154)[[167]](#cite_note-Kaur_2023-167)[[16]](#cite_note-Straumann_2024-16) Due to it being a more potent serotonin 5-HT2A receptor agonist than (*S*)-MDMA, (*R*)-MDMA has been hypothesized to have greater psychedelic effects than (*S*)-MDMA or racemic MDMA.[[209]](#cite_note-Bedi_2024-209)[[16]](#cite_note-Straumann_2024-16) However, this proved not to be the case in a direct clinical comparison of (*R*)-MDMA, (*S*)-MDMA, and racemic MDMA, with equivalent hallucinogen-like effects instead found between the three interventions.[[209]](#cite_note-Bedi_2024-209)[[16]](#cite_note-Straumann_2024-16)

MDMA produces MDA as a minor [active metabolite](/wiki/Active_metabolite "Active metabolite").[[111]](#cite_note-de_la_Torre_2004-111) [Peak levels](/wiki/Cmax_(pharmacology) "Cmax (pharmacology)") of MDA are about 5 to 10% of those of MDMA and [total exposure](/wiki/Area-under-the-curve_(pharmacokinetics) "Area-under-the-curve (pharmacokinetics)") to MDA is almost 10% of that of MDMA with [oral](/wiki/Oral_administration "Oral administration") MDMA administration.[[111]](#cite_note-de_la_Torre_2004-111)[[197]](#cite_note-Tagen_2023-197) As a result, MDA may contribute to some extent to the effects of MDMA.[[111]](#cite_note-de_la_Torre_2004-111)[[188]](#cite_note-Simmler_2018-188) MDA is an entactogen, stimulant, and weak psychedelic similarly to MDMA.[[18]](#cite_note-Oeri_2021-18) Like MDMA, it acts as a potent and well-balanced SNDRA and as a weak serotonin 5-HT2 receptor agonist.[[149]](#cite_note-Rothman_2006-149)[[155]](#cite_note-Setola_2003-155)[[156]](#cite_note-Nash_1994-156) However, MDA shows much more potent and efficacious serotonin 5-HT2A, 5-HT2B, and 5-HT2C receptor agonism than MDMA.[[167]](#cite_note-Kaur_2023-167)[[188]](#cite_note-Simmler_2018-188)[[156]](#cite_note-Nash_1994-156)[[155]](#cite_note-Setola_2003-155) Accordingly, MDA produces greater psychedelic effects than MDMA in humans[[18]](#cite_note-Oeri_2021-18) and might particularly contribute to the mild psychedelic-like effects of MDMA.[[188]](#cite_note-Simmler_2018-188) On the other hand, MDA may also be importantly involved in [toxicity](/wiki/Toxicity "Toxicity") of MDMA, such as [cardiac valvulopathy](/wiki/Cardiac_valvulopathy "Cardiac valvulopathy").[[210]](#cite_note-Luethi_2021-210)[[197]](#cite_note-Tagen_2023-197)[[155]](#cite_note-Setola_2003-155)

The [duration of action](/wiki/Duration_of_action "Duration of action") of MDMA (3–6 hours) is much shorter than its [elimination half-life](/wiki/Elimination_half-life "Elimination half-life") (8–9 hours) would imply.[[211]](#cite_note-Mead_2020-211) In relation to this, MDMA's duration and the offset of its effects appear to be determined more by [rapid acute tolerance](/wiki/Tachyphylaxis "Tachyphylaxis") rather than by circulating drug concentrations.[[43]](#cite_note-Hysek_2012-43) Similar findings have been made for [amphetamine](/wiki/Amphetamine "Amphetamine") and [methamphetamine](/wiki/Methamphetamine "Methamphetamine").[[212]](#cite_note-Ermer_2016-212)[[213]](#cite_note-Cruickshank_2009-213)[[214]](#cite_note-Abbas_2024-214)[[215]](#cite_note-van_Gaalen_2019-215) One mechanism by which [tolerance](/wiki/Drug_tolerance "Drug tolerance") to MDMA may occur is [internalization](/wiki/Endocytosis "Endocytosis") of the [serotonin transporter](/wiki/Serotonin_transporter "Serotonin transporter") (SERT).[[216]](#cite_note-Bisagno_2021-216)[[217]](#cite_note-Kivell_2010-217)[[218]](#cite_note-Holley_2013-218)[[219]](#cite_note-Underhill_2020-219)[[220]](#cite_note-Underhill_2022-220) Although MDMA and serotonin are not significant TAAR1 agonists in humans, TAAR1 activation by MDMA may result in SERT internalization, for instance in rodents in whom MDMA is a potent TAAR1 agonist.[[219]](#cite_note-Underhill_2020-219)[[220]](#cite_note-Underhill_2022-220)[[221]](#cite_note-Kittler_2010-221)[[143]](#cite_note-Gainetdinov_2018-143) It is thought that brain serotonin levels are depleted after MDMA administration but that levels typically return to normal within 24 to 48 hours.[[8]](#cite_note-Betzler_2017-8)
